text: The debate over remote work has dragged on for years, yet the evidence is now overwhelming.
Productivity does not decline when people work from home — in many cases it soars.
Companies that insist on mandatory office attendance are clinging to an outdated norm that prioritises visibility over output.
The workforce has evolved, and rigid policies merely provoke resentment.
Workers value autonomy and the flexibility to integrate professional and personal responsibilities.
Opponents argue that collaboration suffers without physical proximity, but this perspective overlooks the sophisticated tools now available for virtual interaction.
Commuting wastes time, drains energy, and contributes to carbon emissions.
From a strategic standpoint, offering remote options helps firms attract qualified talent from a broader pool.
The transition will not be swift for every industry, but the underlying trend is undeniable.
Organizations that resist this shift do so at their own peril.
